# AgentTrace Performance Benchmarks

**Date:** 2026-06-03  
**Generated by:** packages/sdk/src/benchmark.ts (via `node --expose-gc packages/sdk/dist/benchmark.js`)  
**Environment:** Node.js v22.22.3, linux x64  
**Note:** This document is research-only. Do not commit.

## Executive Summary

Benchmarks cover:

- Trace insertion rate (sequential via public `trace()` API + concurrent)
- Query performance (p50/p95/p99 on 10k trace dataset, various filters)
- Export (JSON/CSV) performance at 1k / 10k / 100k scale
- Memory usage (RSS + V8 heap before/after insert + full materialization)

**Key results (this run):**

- Insertion: ~4,121 traces/sec sequential (p50 0.16 ms, p99 ~2.8 ms); concurrent ~11,210 /sec
- Queries (10k dataset): full scan ~270-650 ms p95 (materializes rows + tool_calls); selective/paginated filters 2-100 ms
- Exports: 1k traces ~20 ms (JSON), 100k ~2.5-3 s; outputs are full fidelity (tens of MB)
- Memory: ~0.0006 MB (~630 bytes) per trace in V8 heap when loaded; inserts primarily use native SQLite memory (RSS delta ~0)

All tests used in-memory SQLite. Results vary run-to-run with system load (prior runs showed up to ~9k insert/sec).

## 1. Trace Insertion Rate

Public API benchmark (5,000 traces): each `await agent.trace(name, fn, {tokens, ...})` — measures wrapper + cost calculation + storage + run stats + optional tool inserts.

| Metric            | Value       |
| ----------------- | ----------- |
| Count             | 5,000       |
| Total time        | 1,213.35 ms |
| Traces per second | 4,121       |

**Per-operation latency (ms):**

| p50  | p95  | p99  | mean | max   |
| ---- | ---- | ---- | ---- | ----- |
| 0.16 | 0.31 | 2.80 | 0.24 | 35.59 |

**Concurrent (Promise.all, 2,000 traces):**

- Time: 178.41 ms
- Traces/sec: 11,210

**Notes:** High throughput for local embedded DB. p99 tail from occasional larger work (tools, stats updates, GC). Concurrent benefits from batching in event loop.

## 2. Query Performance

Dataset: 10,000 traces (populated with varied models, ~5% errors, ~20% tool calls, realistic token/cost/latency/metadata).

Each filter run 50 times; reported mean + percentiles. `getTraces` always materializes full `Trace` objects (incl. subquery per trace for `toolCalls`).

| Name            | Result Count | Mean (ms) | p50 (ms) | p95 (ms) | p99 (ms) | Notes                                 |
| --------------- | ------------ | --------- | -------- | -------- | -------- | ------------------------------------- |
| noFilter        | 10,000       | 335.47    | 270.75   | 650.39   | 755.99   | Full table scan + 10k tool subqueries |
| byStatusSuccess | 9,500        | 224.25    | 214.31   | 298.51   | 365.57   | Status index + majority rows          |
| byStatusError   | 500          | 14.96     | 12.71    | 31.43    | 41.67    | Small result set (status index)       |
| byNameLike      | 3,928        | 85.17     | 87.67    | 102.08   | 109.12   | LIKE on name (no index)               |
| byCostRange     | 7,629        | 194.03    | 171.85   | 353.33   | 429.11   | Cost index helps but still many rows  |
| byLatencyRange  | 4,221        | 88.43     | 89.85    | 101.27   | 103.06   | Latency index                         |
| limitOffset     | 100          | 2.16      | 1.87     | 2.04     | 15.89    | Pagination (LIMIT/OFFSET) — fast      |
| byRunId         | 10,000       | 223.49    | 218.87   | 287.56   | 362.62   | run_id index + full result            |

**Observations:**

- Indexed lookups on small result sets or with LIMIT are sub-50 ms even at p99.
- Full scans or large result sets pay ~20-30 µs/row + tool subquery cost (N+1 pattern in `rowToTrace`).
- Opportunity: could optimize with LEFT JOINs or pre-materialized tool JSON, but current design prioritizes simple schema + FK integrity.

## 3. Export Performance

`agent.export('json' | 'csv')` — full serialization of all traces + embedded tool calls. Single-shot for larger sizes; 10 reps + percentiles for 1k.

| Size    | Format | Mean Time (ms) | Output Length (bytes) | Approx. bytes/trace | p50/p95 (1k only)     |
| ------- | ------ | -------------- | --------------------- | ------------------- | --------------------- |
| 1,000   | JSON   | 19.85          | 677,358               | ~677                | p50 19.75 / p95 20.38 |
| 1,000   | CSV    | 24.04          | 111,720               | ~112                | p50 18.39 / p95 38.98 |
| 10,000  | JSON   | 232.22         | 6,823,703             | ~682                | —                     |
| 10,000  | CSV    | 224.79         | 1,127,281             | ~113                | —                     |
| 100,000 | JSON   | 2,780.33       | 68,741,411            | ~687                | —                     |
| 100,000 | CSV    | 2,560.02       | 11,372,655            | ~114                | —                     |

**Notes:** JSON larger due to nested structure + pretty? (actually compact). CSV more compact. Time roughly linear with size. 100k export in <3s is usable for offline analysis / backup even on large dev datasets.

## 4. Memory Usage

10k trace dataset. Measurements with `--expose-gc` where possible; snapshots around insert and full `getTraces()` materialization.

| Stage               | RSS (MB) | Heap Used (MB) | Delta RSS | Delta Heap |
| ------------------- | -------- | -------------- | --------- | ---------- |
| Before (post-agent) | 538.41   | 4.31           | —         | —          |
| After 10k insert    | 538.41   | 4.27           | 0         | -0.03      |
| After full load     | 538.41   | 10.63          | 0         | +6.32      |

**approxMBPerTrace (heap):** 0.0006 MB (~630 bytes per trace when fully loaded as JS objects)

**Interpretation:**

- Trace data lives primarily in SQLite's native memory (C heap via better-sqlite3) — not duplicated in V8 on insert.
- `getTraces()` materializes full objects (JSON parse, tool subqueries, arrays) → modest V8 heap growth.
- RSS stable; real file-backed DBs would use OS page cache instead of anonymous mappings.
- For apps that rarely call `getTraces()` on huge sets (e.g. stream stats or paginate), memory overhead stays very low.

## 5. Comparison Notes vs Langfuse / LangSmith

These numbers are **not directly comparable** — different architectures and target environments.

**Langfuse (public scale claims from engineering blogs):**

- Post-rearchitecture (event-driven + Redis etc.): "100,000+ events per second with 10-millisecond response times" in massive deployments.
- Tiered: small ~100 eps, medium 1k, large 10k, massive 50k+.
- Ingestion p99 targets <100-500 ms (improved from 50s pre-rearch).
- Self-hosted: requires Docker/K8s; cloud has free tier then paid.
- Emphasis: high-scale multi-tenant observability, prompt mgmt, evals.

**LangSmith:**

- Backed by ClickHouse (chosen for high-throughput ingest + low-latency analytical queries/charts).
- SDK batch ingestion (multipart) for higher throughput / rate limits; designed so tracing adds negligible app latency.
- Scale evidence: "15 billion+ traces" processed historically across customers.
- Cloud-only (no self-host); free 5k traces/mo then usage + seat pricing.
- Strong for LangGraph production; enterprise SLAs.

**AgentTrace (this benchmark):**

- Local embedded (better-sqlite3, WAL, single-process, :memory: or file). No network, auth, serialization to remote, containers.
- Achieves several thousand inserts/sec (4k–11k observed) with sub-millisecond p50/p99 insert latency on commodity hardware.
- Query/export perf suitable for local dev, debugging, small-medium datasets, or edge/server local logging.
- Full data never leaves machine unless explicitly exported.
- Tradeoffs: full materialization queries on 10k+ rows are 100s of ms (N+1 tools); not sharded/OLAP-optimized for 100k+ eps or petabyte analytics.
- Best fit: privacy-sensitive, zero-config, CLI-first, local-first agent development (complements rather than replaces cloud platforms).

**When to choose which (perf angle):**

- Need <10 ms p99 ingestion at 10k+ eps under bursty multi-user load, team features, hosted UI → Langfuse cloud or self-host.
- LangGraph-heavy prod, need massive historical analytics + ClickHouse power, don't mind cloud billing → LangSmith.
- Local/privacy, want instant `npm install`, SQLite you can `sqlite3` or query from app/CLI, costs auto-tracked, no infra → AgentTrace (this project). Scale horizontally by sharding DB files per tenant/process if needed; or export OTEL to your existing stack.

See also [docs/comparison.md](../comparison.md) for broader (non-perf) feature matrix.

## 6. How to Reproduce

```bash
# From repo root (after any src changes)
pnpm --filter "./packages/sdk" run build
node --expose-gc packages/sdk/dist/benchmark.js
# (or for src direct with type stripping — note ESM import .js extensions may require additional loader)
node --experimental-strip-types packages/sdk/src/benchmark.ts
```

Output is JSON (machine readable). The script is intentionally not a vitest (avoids test timeouts on large exports).
